hero.tagline: The goal of our work is to centre the human experiences and values to improve equity and thoughtful, meaningful, and inclusive technology use in healthcare.
hero.land_acknowledgement: Racialized settler on the unceded and traditional territories of the Syilx Okanagan

director.name: Charlene E. Ronquillo
director.title: Assistant Professor
director.department: School of Nursing, Faculty of Health and Social Development
director.address: ART360 (Arts Building)\n1147 Research Road\nKelowna, BC Canada V1V 1V7
bio: The HIE Lab's work is interdisciplinary and underpinned by critical theories and implementation science. A key aim in our research is to ensure meaningful inclusion of non-dominant groups in the conceptualization, design, development, and implementation of health technologies in health systems.

Charlene has expertise in mixed methods, participatory, and co-production approaches, HCI, and employing critical perspectives throughout the technology lifecycle. Charlene has experience with various aspects of health information technology in health systems and nursing, including nursing informatics competencies, usability research, person-centred design and development, rapid and exploratory prototyping, and technology adoption and usage.

active.title: LANTERN: Learning and AI Navigation for Transformative Education and Research in Nursing
active.co_investigators: Burton, L., Douglas-Campbell, K., Harvey, C., Lawrence, R., Munro, T., Nanyonjo, J., Rush, K., Wilson, R.
active.description: The LANTERN project addresses the unique needs of graduate nursing students by developing educator-designed and controlled AI agent ensemble to foster development of critical thinking through Socratic dialogue.

active.title: Thinking Aloud with AI: Nursing Students' Cognitive Processes Using GenAI for Clinical Reasoning
active.co_investigators: Wilson, R., Willson, M., Garcia Chua, R., Moralejo, L., Kennedy L., Sturgess, K.
active.description: This project aims to understand cognitive processes involved when undergraduate nursing students use generative AI tools when working on complex clinical reasoning tasks.

active.title: Stakeholder perspectives and impacts of explainable artificial intelligence: A case study in a British Columbia health authority
active.funding: SSHRC Insight Development Grant (2023–2025); UBC HIFI Awards (2022–23)
active.co_investigators: Conati, C., Currie, L.M., Hamarneh, G., Janke, R., Li, X., Block, L., Beschastnikh, I.
active.description: This study employs realist methods to explore considerations for health systems operations and clinician end-user implications of developing and implementing explainable AI and clinical decision support tools.

active.title: Mapping trust in nurses with dimensions of trustworthy artificial intelligence: A scoping review
active.co_investigators: Booth, R., Janke, R., Chan, R., Recsky, C.
active.description: Is it possible for artificial intelligence (AI) models to demonstrate trustworthiness in ways that the world's consistently most trusted professionals – nurses – are thought to do? Or is it that some dimensions of trustworthiness are only demonstrable by humans? This scoping review and thematic analysis will map and synthesize the evidence on nursing trust and understand parallels to computational understandings of trustworthiness, in order to envision novel approaches to developing trustworthy AI.

team.description: Above all, we value doing good work with good people. We work as a collaborative team grounded in compassion, justice, equity, humility, diversity, inclusion, transparency, and interdisciplinarity. We value the unique and diverse contributions each person brings, work on interesting problems to help technology be used for good, and strive to develop thoughtful and inclusive technologies that are meaningful and useful to people.

current.name: Dr. Chantelle Recsky
current.bio: Dr. Recsky is a Registered Nurse and her areas of interest include health information technologies, quality and safety, and knowledge translation, as well as supporting students to gain knowledge and experience in research.

current.name: Andrea Burrows
current.bio: Andrea is a Registered Nurse and PhD candidate in the Faculty of Health and Social Development at the University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus. Her doctoral research explores how nurses use digital technologies to provide individualised care for families in enhanced family visiting programs from across Canada. Andrea holds a Masters of Science in Nursing from UBC and a Bachelors of Science in Nursing from the University of Alberta. Outside her research, Andrea enjoys spending time with her family.

current.name: Naureen Mukhi
current.bio: Naureen Mukhi is a Registered Nurse and Graduate Research Assistant working on her Masters in Nursing at the University of British Columbia (Okanagan). Her thesis is on investigating health system stakeholders' perspectives use of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) for clinical decision support. Naureen has recently completed her diploma in Digital Health and Data Analytics from the Michener Institute of Education in Ontario. In parallel with her studies, she also works as Clinical Informatics Specialist with the Provincial Public Health Information System team in BC. Naureen enjoys nature, running and spends time volunteering in her community prayer place.

current.name: Josephine Nanyonjo
current.bio: Josephine is deeply committed to addressing the persistent health inequities that exist globally. As a Registered Nurse, she has dedicated the past few years to improving healthcare in rural and underserved areas of Uganda, utilizing her knowledge, skills, and time to make a tangible impact. She is particularly enthusiastic about the potential of technology to provide innovative solutions to this enduring global challenge. Josephine is a social person who values spending time with friends and family, and enjoys swimming, hiking and spending time in nature.

current.name: Kathryn Sturgess
current.bio: Kathryn Sturgess is a fourth year nursing student at UBC Okanagan, originally from Canmore, Alberta. She is interested in how artificial intelligence can be used in healthcare, with a focus on its ethical use and practical application in clinical settings. Looking ahead, Kathryn hopes to pursue a career in medicine and research, specifically within rural emergency departments.

current.name: Amrit Kaur Dhami
current.bio: Amrit Kaur Dhami is currently in her fourth year of nursing and thrilled to be a part of this research team. She is deeply committed to fostering a supportive and inclusive community within the team. She believes that research provides an excellent opportunity to contribute to the enhancement of nursing experiences, advocate for the needs of our peers, and facilitate meaningful connections among students, faculty, and the broader nursing community. With her strong interpersonal skills, leadership experience, and genuine enthusiasm for the occupation she is dedicated to working as a team member. She loves spending her summers in Kelowna and enjoying the warm weather, beaches, and good company.

current.name: Jianan Wang
current.bio: Jianan is a nursing PhD student from Zhejiang University, China, and is currently a visiting international research student at UBCO. She received her B.S. in Nursing from Beijing University of Chinese Medicine, China. Her research focuses on the ethical implications of information technology in the nursing field, with a particular interest in understanding how these technologies influence nurses' behaviors.

current.name: Dr. Hilal Yildirim
current.bio: Dr Hilal Yıldırım is a Visiting Scientist at the University of British Columbia Okanagan, working under the supervision of Charlene Ronquillo, whose work in digital health and health equity has been highly influential in shaping her current research direction. She is an Associate Professor in the Department of Public Health Nursing at Inonu University, Türkiye, where she continues her academic and research activities. Dr. Yıldırım has been actively engaged in academia since 2011, following her clinical experience in hospital settings between 2009 and 2011. With over a decade of experience, her work focuses on public health nursing, social determinants of health, and vulnerable populations, with a growing emphasis on healthy aging and equitable access to care. Her current research centers on digital health and remote care technologies for older adults, particularly in disaster-affected and resource-constrained settings. She is especially interested in how innovative, technology-supported approaches can enhance accessibility, usability, and continuity of care for aging populations within community and public health systems. As part of her collaboration in Canada, she contributes to research aligned with health equity and digital transformation in healthcare, aiming to develop scalable and context-sensitive solutions that respond to real-world needs. Her work bridges clinical insight, public health priorities, and emerging digital health innovations. Her recent collaborative publication examines the effectiveness of eHealth approaches for blood pressure monitoring and control in older adults, reflecting her commitment to advancing evidence-based digital health practices. Selected Publication: Yıldırım, H., Borycki, E., Mallidou, A. A., Dordunoo, D., & Yıldız, S. (2026). Examining the effectiveness of eHealth approaches to blood pressure monitoring and control in older adults: A systematic review. Educational Gerontology, 1–17. ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3971-3799 | ResearchGate: https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Hilal-Yildirim-7/research | Web of Science: https://www.webofscience.com/wos/author/record/ABI-6245-2020.

current.name: Nicole Ketter
current.bio: Nicole is currently working on her BScN at the University of Calgary. She previously completed her BSc in Biology from UBCO and her MSc in Rehabilitation Sciences from UBC. She has been part of the HIE lab since 2022 and enjoys contributing to developing knowledge on responsible AI use.

current.name: Diana Shim
current.bio: Diana Shim is a third-year nursing student at UBC Okanagan. She has clinical experience as an Employed Student Nurse, has contributed to the Interior Health New Grad Steering Committee, and has supported students as a simulation lab monitor. She is excited to develop her skills in academic writing, literature reviews, qualitative research, and technology-enabled equity work. Outside of academics, Diana enjoys spending time with friends, baking, and cooking.

news.text: We're excited to welcome Dr. Hilal Yildirim from Inonu University (Turkey) as a visiting scientist for one year.
Charlene was elected President-Elect of the Canadian Nursing Informatics Association (CNIA).
The lab launched the Thinking Aloud with AI study, exploring how nursing students use generative AI for clinical reasoning, supported by a UBC SoTL Seed Grant.
New publication in Journal of Nursing Scholarship: The Overlooked Dark Side of Generative AI in Nursing.
Congratulations to Amrit and Kate for their fantastic presentation at WRCASN titled, "Student Research Experiences in the Integration of AI Technologies and Its Relevance to Nursing Education."
We're excited to welcome Dr. Clarissa Bohrer da Silva as a visiting postdoc. She joins us from the Universidade do Estado de Santa Catarina in Chapecó, Brazil.
Naureen is awarded a SSHRC CGS-M Award for her Masters thesis work. Congratulations!
Andrea completes a major program milestone and achieves PhD Candidacy. Congratulations!
Charlene and the NAIL Collaborative host the Embedding nursing expertise in artificial intelligence-based health technology development workshop at Fondation Brocher in Hermance, Switzerland.
Charlene gave a keynote at the 2024 Health Research and AI: A CMDO Symposium in Montreal, QC.
Charlene gave her first international keynote and presented a paper and 2 posters at NI2024 in Manchester, UK.
Charlene and members of the NAIL Collaborative were invited as experts to the "Artificial Intelligence in Reality (AIR) Roundtable for Nursing and Midwifery," organized by NHS England.
Charlene is Co-I on a newly funded UBCO Eminence Research Cluster: Data Safety and AI Literacy (2024–27).
Two new publications: Evaluating the Representation of Disaster Hazards in SNOMED CT (JAMIA) and Differing Effects of Implementation Leadership on Nurses' Use of mHealth (JMIR Nursing).
Charlene and the NAIL Collaborative are awarded Artificial Intelligence Journal (Elsevier) funding for the workshop on Ethical, Social, and Legal Implications of Using Generative AI in Healthcare.
Sharon, Nicole, and Charlene give 5 presentations at CNIA 2023 (Calgary, AB) and at the International Council of Nurses Congress 2023 (Montreal, QC).
Charlene and Dr. Xiaoxiao Li are awarded the UBC Collaborative Research Mobility Award to support collaboration across the UBC Vancouver and Okanagan campuses.
Our team is awarded a Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council of Canada (SSHRC) Insight Development Grant.
Charlene receives the 2023 UBC School of Nursing Recent Alumni Award.
Charlene and the NAIL Collaborative receive support from Fondation Brocher (Hermance, Switzerland) to host the nursing AI workshop in November 2024.

opportunities.intro: Across all levels of collaboration and supervision, I encourage applicants from underrepresented groups in nursing research and academia to apply to work with me.
opportunities.collaboration_text: I welcome inquiries from health authorities, health systems stakeholders, NGOs, communities/community-organisations, and industry interested in health equity and health technologies to email me (healthinformatics.equity@ubc.ca) if you would like to work together.
opportunities.postdoc_text: Local and remote collaborations are welcomed. Please email me.
opportunities.grad_status: I am not accepting graduate students for the 2025 and 2026 academic intakes.
opportunities.grad_general: Applying to pursue graduate studies is not a decision to take lightly. Preparing a strong graduate application package takes time and initial relationship building with your potential supervisor. As a general guide, I do not entertain supervision inquiries made within 1 month of the application deadline.
opportunities.grad_interest: I am keen to mentor and support high-calibre thesis-stream Masters and PhD students who have a strong interest in research in the field of nursing and health informatics.

grad_programs.name: UBC Interdisciplinary Graduate Studies (IGS) Global Studies Stream
UBCO School of Nursing Graduate Programs

phd_requirements: You should have a foundational understanding of nursing/health informatics (courses or work experience)
Already obtained a Master's degree in nursing, health informatics, or a related field
Published at least one peer-reviewed paper in a health informatics journal
opportunities.undergrad_text: I encourage UBCO Nursing students interested in completing a Y4 research preceptorship to contact me. I occasionally offer funded research internships, work study, or co-op positions.
application_requirements: A short description of the project you are interested in and how your background aligns with the project aims
Your background in health or nursing informatics, including supporting publications (required for PhD applicants)
Your CV and copy of a recent publication or other writing sample
Please put "[HIE!Lab]" in your subject line

advisory.intro: The HIE PIE (Patient/Provider/Process Involvement Engagement) Advisory is a group of patient partners, healthcare providers, and process advisors who collaborate meaningfully in HIE Lab research. Advisory members contribute to research questions, priorities, study design, and knowledge translation — ensuring our work is grounded in lived experience and real-world practice.

members.name: Lyn Brooks
members.bio: Lyn is the Founder of dHub Group, an eMHIC Ambassador, and a member of the AI Ethics Advisory Panel at the Digital Governance Council in Canada. He serves as a Board Director, Advisory Council Member, and Ambassador for Ayra.forum. He is a passionate advocate for data sovereignty by design, for-profit community data commons, platform cooperativism, and the alignment of technology with humanity.

members.name: Chris J
members.bio: Lifelong experience of complex chronic illness motivates Chris to participate as a patient partner in a broad range of research, quality improvement, health technology assessment, evidence syntheses, standards development and governance initiatives. With a background in instructional design, web technologies and learning management, Chris has a passion for continuous learning. She has a keen interest in health technologies including AI, digital health, virtual care, remote monitoring, health data literacy, interoperability, and learning health systems.

members.name: Katie Tong
members.bio: Katie is a long-time resident of the Greater Vancouver area. She began her journey as a patient partner in 2019 and has remained actively engaged in healthcare initiatives ever since.

members.name: Michel White
members.bio: Michel is a patient partner who had surgery over 40 years ago on a large adenoma on her pituitary gland. Tumor grew back and she had six weeks of radiation therapy. She now has panhypopituitarism. Michel has also had both knees replaced and a meningioma removed. Michel is also a caregiver as her daughter had a double lung transplant three years ago. She is much more than her medical condition, speaking for herself and other patients who can’t or don’t know how to speak for themselves. She has been involved in many committees over the last twenty-five years that she has been a patient partner, including two cohorts with the Physician Quality Improvement and then the steering committee. Every day is a gift. Yesterday is gone, it’s history. Tomorrow isn’t here yet, it’s a mystery. That’s why today is called The Present – it’s a gift – unwrap it carefully and be Thankful for every day.
